rules.condition: [Patch rule — melanoma_vs_melanocytic_nevus] Dominant color is a single reddish-brown to dark-brown hue without true polychromia (no gray, blue, white, or black zones); Pigment distribution, while non-uniform in texture, lacks discrete structureless zones or focal color breaks indicative of true architectural disorder; Borders fade gradually into surrounding skin (soft, moth-eaten periphery) rather than ending abruptly or showing notching; No blue-white veil (confluent blue-grey haze over raised area); No regression structures: no white scar-like areas, no blue-grey peppering; No atypical dots or globules at the periphery that are markedly irregular in size and distribution; Overall lesion shape is roughly symmetric on at least one axis; If a pigment network is present, it is regular (uniform mesh with consistent line thickness and hole size) rather than atypical; network lines do not show focal thickening, branching streaks, or abrupt peripheral termination; No radial streaming or pseudopods projecting asymmetrically at the periphery; No milky-red areas or irregular vascular structures (dotted, irregular, or polymorphous vessels) inconsistent with a benign nevus; Lesion size is within a range that does not by itself raise strong suspicion for melanoma; no pronounced focal elevation or nodular component suggesting dermal invasion; No satellite lesions or discrete pigmented foci clearly separate from the main lesion body
rules.action: Classify as Melanocytic Nevus. Confidence: moderate. Rule: When a lesion shows a predominantly homogeneous reddish-brown to dark-brown diffuse pigmentation with a roughly circular to oval overall silhouette, gradual peripheral fading (moth-eaten or fading border rather than sharp cut-off), no discrete structureless white areas, no regression structures (white scar-like areas or blue-grey peppering), no atypical pigment network with abrupt termination, and no blue-white veil, classify as Melanocytic Nevus.
